how_it_works: Each carrier server (twilio.ts:116, telnyx.ts:135, smartpbx.ts:109) builds a TransportAdapter<State> and runs it through the shared runWebSocketConnection host (transport-host.ts:102). Inbound JSON frames are parsed, validated, base64-decoded (transport-helpers.ts:64 decodeStrictBase64), codec-decoded to Int16 PCM (mu-law/L16/pcm16/opus), resampled via a per-direction stateful resampler map (resample.ts:162), and pushed to the bus as user.audio_received on Route.Main (twilio.ts:308, telnyx.ts:586 emitTelnyxMediaFrame, smartpbx.ts:275). Telnyx additionally runs a bounded reorder buffer keyed by media.chunk (telnyx.ts:525-576) that force-flushes and gap-skips when it exceeds maxInboundReorderFrames (default 4). Outbound TTS audio flows through wireTelephonyOutboundPipeline (outbound-playout-pipeline.ts:27): tts.audio is sliced into 20ms frames by each carrier's encodeFrames, enqueued into a PacedPlayoutQueue capped at maxQueuedOutputAudioMs (now defaulted to 200ms after VE-01.3 — smartpbx.ts:102 was just lowered from 30000), and paced out; interrupt.tts clears the interruptible queue and calls onInterrupt which sends carrier 'clear' for Twilio (twilio.ts:250) and Telnyx (telnyx.ts:265) but only emits a metric for SmartPBX (smartpbx.ts:193, protocol has no clear). DTMF is parsed (parseDtmfDigit, digits 0-9 * #) and pushed on Route.Critical as a typed dtmf.received packet (twilio.ts:352, telnyx.ts:372, smartpbx.ts:305) with provider+rawDigit; no engine subscriber maps it to interrupt/STT/VAD (verified: packets.ts:250, packet-factories.ts:49 are the only references), so DTMF correctly bypasses the speech path.

findings.title: DTMF received before 'start' emits dtmf.received with empty contextId (no started/contextId guard)
findings.evidence: The DTMF branch only guards `if (state.stopped) return;` and then pushes `{ kind: "dtmf.received", contextId: state.contextId, ... }`. Before a valid start, `state.contextId` is the createState default "" (twilio.ts:146-147). Contrast the media branch which explicitly guards `if (!state.started || !state.contextId) throw new Error("Twilio media event received before a valid start event");` (twilio.ts:300). So a `dtmf` frame arriving before/instead-of `start` produces a Route.Critical packet with an empty contextId that downstream consumers keyed by contextId cannot route, while media in the same state is rejected. No test covers pre-start DTMF (grep for dtmf-before-start returns nothing).
findings.recommendation: Mirror the media-event guard in all three DTMF handlers: `if (!state.started || !state.contextId) throw new Error("<carrier> dtmf event received before a valid start event");` (or silently drop with a metric). Add a per-carrier test sending dtmf before start and asserting no dtmf.received with empty contextId is emitted.
findings.checklist_ref: PRODUCTION-CHECKLIST.md §9 (line 89: route DTMF as a typed control event) + §1 (line 65: carrier JSON envelopes are correctness contracts; stream identity/context on every frame)

findings.title: Telnyx throws (and sends a carrier error frame) on a stale/duplicate media.chunk, which is a NORMAL occurrence under carrier reordering
findings.evidence: `if (frame.chunk < state.nextInboundMediaChunk) { throw new Error(\`Telnyx media.chunk is stale or duplicated: expected at least ${...}, received ${...}\`); }` and the same throw for an already-buffered chunk. This throw propagates to handleMessage -> adapter.sendError, which emits an `error` frame back to Telnyx (telnyx.ts:677). Telnyx's own docs state "The order of events is not guaranteed and the chunk number can be used to reorder the events" (https://developers.telnyx.com/docs/voice/programmable-voice/media-streaming) and that DTMF "may arrive out of order" — so a retransmitted/late chunk after the reorder buffer already gap-skipped past it (telnyx.ts:571 advances nextInboundMediaChunk to lowestBufferedChunk) is expected, not malformed. The test `rejects duplicate Telnyx media chunks` (telnyx.test.ts:308-351) bakes in the error-frame behavior. Already-delivered audio is not lost and the socket is not closed, so impact is bounded to error-frame noise + per-duplicate CPU/log churn, but a reordering carrier will spam error frames mid-call.
findings.recommendation: Treat stale/duplicate chunks as a normal-reorder condition: drop the frame and emit a Background metric (e.g. telnyx.media_chunk_duplicate / telnyx.media_chunk_stale) instead of throwing + sending a carrier error frame. Reserve errors for genuinely malformed envelopes. Update the test to assert a dropped-duplicate metric rather than an error frame.
findings.checklist_ref: PRODUCTION-CHECKLIST.md §1 (line 11/55: bounded jitter buffering and stateful edge handling); VE-04 bridge “Carrier DTMF/media schemas differ; ... retain raw provider metadata” / reorder buffer

findings.title: SmartPBX interrupt does not flush carrier-buffered audio (no clear-on-interrupt), so already-delivered frames keep playing over the user
findings.evidence: SmartPBX onInterrupt only emits `name: "smartpbx.interrupt_no_playback_clear"` and does NOT send any carrier control message, unlike Twilio (`event: "clear", streamSid`) and Telnyx (`event: "clear"`). The pipeline does call `playout.clearInterruptible()` (outbound-playout-pipeline.ts:86) so the SERVER stops sending new frames, but any frames already accepted by SmartPBX's jitter/playout buffer cannot be flushed — on barge-in the assistant talks over the user until that buffer drains. This is an inherent SmartPBX protocol limitation (the metric name documents it), not a coding mistake, but the checklist invariant 'interruption emits clear before next response audio' is not fully met for this carrier.
findings.recommendation: Confirm with the SmartPBX integration whether any flush/clear/abort control exists; if so, send it on interrupt. If not, keep the metric but document the residual talk-over window in the telephony integration docs and minimize it by keeping maxQueuedOutputAudioMs low (already 200ms) so server-side queued audio is small. Add a test asserting the interrupt metric fires and the interruptible queue is cleared.
findings.checklist_ref: PRODUCTION-CHECKLIST.md §1 (lines 65-67: clear-on-interrupt; interruption emits clear before next response audio)

findings.title: dtmf_bypasses_speech_path tests assert the speech-path set is empty only after waiting for dtmf.received, not after a settle window
findings.evidence: `await waitForCondition(() => dtmfReceived.length === 1); ... expect(speechPath).toEqual([]);` — the assertion that no interrupt.tts/vad/stt packet was emitted runs as soon as the dtmf.received packet lands. dtmf.received is on Route.Critical and would land first; a (hypothetical regression) interrupt emitted slightly later on a different route could arrive after the assertion and be missed. The test correctly subscribes to 10 speech kinds incl interrupt.tts (a genuinely strong shape), but the timing makes the negative assertion slightly under-determined.
findings.recommendation: After dtmfReceived resolves, add a short settle (e.g. await a ~20-30ms timer or a tts.audio round-trip) before asserting speechPath is empty, so a late-emitted interrupt would be caught.
findings.checklist_ref: PRODUCTION-CHECKLIST.md §9 (line 89-91: DTMF must not trigger barge-in); VE-04 bridge acceptance “prove no interrupt.tts appears”

findings.title: Reported smartpbx malformed-base64 flake does not reproduce at HEAD; real flakiness is in graceful-drain and browser-pacing (timeout-based)
findings.evidence: Per the domain note the malformed-base64 test was ~1/5 flaky. I could not reproduce it: it passed 20/20 in isolation, 30/30 running the full smartpbx file, and 8/8 reading its verbose result inside full-suite runs (66+ executions, 0 failures). The malformed path is deterministic (decodeStrictBase64('not base64') fails len%4 and the charset regex, confirmed) and the server does NOT close the socket on a processMessage throw (transport-host.ts:155-157 only calls sendError), so there is no server-close-vs-error-frame race. Running the FULL package suite, however, DID flake 2/10 — both failures were Test-timed-out in `graceful connection draining > non-graceful close terminates all clients immediately` and `WT-03 Browser outbound pacing > respects custom outbound frame duration and queue limits`, i.e. transport-core/browser tests, not the telephony serializers. The just-applied fix (smartpbx.ts DEFAULT_MAX_QUEUED_OUTPUT_AUDIO_MS 30000->200, plus per-test maxQueuedOutputAudioMs overrides at smartpbx.test.ts:491,555) changed outbound-pacing timing and likely shifted/closed the old malformed-base64 flake window.
findings.recommendation: Re-attribute the flaky-test follow-up: stabilize graceful-drain.test.ts and browser-pacing.test.ts (timeout-driven, sensitive to event-loop load) rather than smartpbx malformed-base64. If a deterministic gate is needed, raise those tests' timeouts or make their close/pacing waits condition-based rather than wall-clock. These two tests are outside the telephony-serializer domain (transport-core) — route to that owner.
findings.checklist_ref: PRODUCTION-CHECKLIST.md §1 (carrier serializer test reliability); VE-04 bridge acceptance test stability

summary: The telephony serializers are SOLID at the core: mu-law/L16/pcm16/opus decode+encode, per-direction stateful resamplers (bounded, not unbounded), 20ms outbound framing, paced playout with the VE-01.3 200ms queue cap, stream identity on every Twilio outbound media/control frame (streamSid), and a correct Telnyx reorder buffer. DTMF routing is well done and meets the VE-04 bridge's central acceptance: dtmf.received is a typed Route.Critical packet with provider+rawDigit and has NO engine subscriber that triggers interrupt/STT/VAD (verified across packages/voice), so DTMF genuinely bypasses the speech path. I confirmed against live Telnyx docs that omitting stream_id on outbound media/clear/mark is correct (Telnyx outbound frames carry no stream_id), so that is not a bug. Open concerns: (1) DTMF before a valid start emits a packet with empty contextId in all three serializers while media in the same state is rejected — a real contract inconsistency; (2) Telnyx throws and sends a carrier error frame on stale/duplicate media.chunk, which is normal under the reordering the carrier explicitly documents; (3) SmartPBX interrupt sends no carrier clear (protocol limitation) so the checklist clear-on-interrupt invariant is only partially met there. On the flake: the reported smartpbx malformed-base64 flake did not reproduce in 66+ runs at this working tree (the path is deterministic and the server does not close on the error), but I DID reproduce full-suite flakiness (2/10) in graceful-drain and browser-pacing timeout tests — the follow-up should be re-pointed there (transport-core domain), not at the malformed-base64 telephony test.
